seoTitle: How to Add Sum Calculations in a Confluence Table
metaDescription: Calculate column totals inside Confluence using the native Databases feature or the Elements Spreadsheet macro, without copying data to Excel.
intro: Copying numbers out to an external spreadsheet just to get a total defeats the purpose of documenting them in Confluence in the first place. This guide covers two ways to add automatic sum calculations to a Confluence table — the native Databases feature and the Elements Spreadsheet macro.

steps.heading: Insert a Confluence Database object
steps.body: Add a Confluence Database block onto your page canvas instead of a basic static table.

steps.heading: Set numeric field types
steps.body: Configure the relevant columns to a Number data type and input your project data.

steps.heading: Open the calculate panel
steps.body: Hover below your value column and click the native Calculate button that appears.

steps.heading: Select Sum
steps.body: Choose the Sum option from the dropdown to instantly display the column total.

steps.heading: Or use the spreadsheet macro
steps.body: Launch the macro search tool and choose Elements Spreadsheet for a more Excel-like layout with sigma-based formulas.

steps.heading: Publish your totals
steps.body: Click the blue Publish button to push the automated totals live for your team.

faq.question: Do I need a third-party app to sum a Confluence table?
faq.answer: Not necessarily — Confluence's native Databases feature includes a built-in Calculate option, though marketplace apps like Elements Spreadsheet offer more advanced formulas.

faq.question: Will the sum update automatically if I edit a value?
faq.answer: Yes, since it's a live calculation, changing a number in the column should update the displayed total.

faq.question: Can I calculate more than just a sum?
faq.answer: The native Calculate panel offers a dropdown of options beyond just Sum, and spreadsheet-style macros support broader Excel-like functions.

faq.question: Why isn't my Calculate button showing up?
faq.answer: This usually means the column isn't formatted as a Number data type, which is required for the calculation tools to appear.
